narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the effort you've put into your law school dreams, and I want to walk you through why our predictive model saw your path to the bar exam as riskier than we'd like—landing at a 45.8% chance of not passing. Picture it like a story the model pieced together from your profile: your Year 1 grades placing you in the 4th decile stood out as the biggest red flag, suggesting an early struggle to keep pace that often foreshadows tougher battles ahead. Layered on that, your LSAT score of 39 echoed a similar concern, pulling the prediction firmly toward failure by hinting at challenges in the high-stakes reasoning the bar demands. Your lower-middle family income added another layer, as the model has seen how that bracket can limit access to the extra tutoring or support that turns close calls into successes. Even your Year 3 decile of 1, while showing some grit in climbing ranks, wasn't enough to offset those early hurdles in the model's eyes. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.3 fit into this narrative too, reinforcing a pattern of academic hurdles rather than a clear upward trajectory. Elements like your white racial background and full-time student status nudged things slightly toward risk, based on patterns from past students, though your gender actually worked in your favor, tilting gently toward passing. Together, these pieces painted a picture of someone with heart and full-time commitment—targeting law school head-on—but facing an uphill climb against odds the model couldn't ignore. It's not a judgment on your potential; it's just the story these factors told. If you boost that early academic foundation or LSAT prep, you could rewrite this narrative entirely. You've got this—keep pushing.
